ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Xinjiang Big Plate Chicken (Da Pan Ji). Mapped to the broader 'chicken dish' class (FOODON_00002572) as the closest defensible ancestor. A more specific term for braised Chinese chicken noodle dishes does not currently exist in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat dish — composite prepared dish with poultry, vegetables, and starchy noodles
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (composite poultry dish) used as the closest defensible match. No specific FoodEx2 entry exists for Xinjiang-style braised chicken with noodles. Composite dish classification applied per FoodEx2 hierarchy for mixed meat-starch-vegetable preparations.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept found for Xinjiang Big Plate Chicken or equivalent composite Chinese noodle-chicken dish. SNOMED CT does not index this regional dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical individual serving of Beijiang Chicken (Boneless) is approximately 350g, comprising a generous portion of braised boneless chicken pieces with potatoes, bell peppers, and a serving of wide flat noodles, as commonly plated at Singapore Chinese restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat and skin, cooked, stewed (FDC ID 171477) combined with USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked noodles (FDC ID 20121), cooked potato (FDC ID 170026), and bell pepper (FDC ID 170108); weighted composite estimate for Xinjiang-style Big Plate Chicken dish
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore entry exists for Xinjiang Big Plate Chicken (Da Pan Ji). Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for stewed chicken (FDC ID 171477, ~40% by weight), cooked wide noodles (FDC ID 20121, ~30%), cooked potato (FDC ID 170026, ~15%), and bell pepper (FDC ID 170108, ~10%), with soy sauce and oil accounting for the remaining ~5%. The input record values (energy 185 kcal, protein 12g, fat 8.5g, carbohydrate 15g) are broadly consistent with this composite estimate and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 350mg/100g is plausible given soy sauce content. All values cross-checked against the input AI-generated record and found to be within defensible range.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Analogous estimate based on boiled white noodles (GI ~53–65) and boiled potato (GI ~78), weighted by dish composition.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Xinjiang Big Plate Chicken (Da Pan Ji). GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on weighted composite of the two primary carbohydrate sources: wide flat noodles (GI ~53–65, analogous to boiled white noodles per University of Sydney GI Database) and boiled potato (GI ~78 per University of Sydney GI Database), weighted approximately 65% noodles and 35% potato by carbohydrate contribution. Protein (12g/100g) and fat (8.5g/100g) from chicken and oil moderate the overall glycaemic response. Glycaemic load per 350g serving = (GI 65 × carbohydrate_g per serving 26.25g) / 100 = 17.1 (medium GL). Estimation basis documented per Veritas GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 350mg per 100g from soy sauce, dark soy sauce, and added salt; a 350g serving delivers approximately 1,225mg sodium, representing over 50% of the 2,300mg daily limit in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and salt during preparation, limit portion size to 200g or less, and increase the proportion of bell peppers and onions to boost potassium and partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Dual high-GI carbohydrate load from wide flat noodles (GI ~65) and boiled potato (GI ~78) with low dietary fibre (1.5g/100g), producing a medium-to-high glycaemic load of approximately 17 per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request a reduced noodle and potato portion, substitute wide noodles with a lower-GI alternative such as konjac or wholegrain noodles, and retain the chicken protein component which partially moderates postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.0g/100g and cholesterol 45mg/100g from chicken skin and cooking oil, combined with high sodium (350mg/100g) which elevates blood pressure risk; trans fat is low at 0.05g/100g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request skinless chicken, reduced cooking oil, and lower soy sauce quantities, and limit portion to 250g to keep saturated fat below 5g and sodium below 875mg per meal occasion.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Bell peppers, dried chilies, and potatoes are nightshade vegetables that may trigger solanine-mediated joint inflammation in RA-sensitive individuals; refined carbohydrate load from noodles and potatoes also promotes pro-inflammatory glycaemic response.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who are sensitive to nightshades should reduce or eliminate bell peppers, dried chilies, and potatoes from the dish, retain ginger and cumin for their anti-inflammatory gingerol and terpenoid content, and request a smaller noodle portion to reduce glycaemic load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beijiang Chicken (Boneless), known in Chinese as 大盘鸡（去骨）(Dà Pán Jī Qù Gǔ) or 'Big Plate Chicken Boneless', is a Xinjiang-style Chinese braised chicken dish widely served in Singapore Chinese restaurants. The dish features tender boneless chicken pieces — typically from thigh and drumstick — slow-stewed with potato chunks, bell peppers, dried red chilies, ginger, garlic, cumin, Sichuan peppercorn, and star anise in a rich soy-based braising sauce. It is served over or alongside wide, chewy flat noodles (liang pi or belt noodles) that absorb the deeply spiced, savoury braising liquid. The flavour profile is bold, aromatic, mildly spicy, and umami-rich, with the chicken yielding a tender, fall-apart texture and the potatoes providing a soft, starchy contrast. Nutritionally, per 100g the dish provides approximately 185 kcal, 12g protein, 8.5g fat (2g saturated), and 15g carbohydrate with 1.5g dietary fibre and 350mg sodium. The dual carbohydrate load from noodles and potatoes produces a medium-to-high glycaemic index (estimated GI 65) and a glycaemic load of approximately 17 per 350g serving, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The high sodium content from soy sauce and dark soy sauce is a concern for hypertension management. The dish is halal-suitable when prepared without alcohol, dairy-free, and contains gluten from the wheat noodles. It is commonly ordered as a shared main course for two to three people in Singapore, with a full serving weighing approximately 1,000g.

Is Beijiang Chicken suitable for diabetics?

What noodles are used in Da Pan Ji?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central) contains a direct entry for Xinjiang Big Plate Chicken (Da Pan Ji). Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for stewed chicken, cooked noodles, cooked potato, and bell pepper. The input AI-generated values are broadly consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for the component carbohydrate sources. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite braised chicken and noodle dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite Xinjiang-style dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16; individual ingredient entries exist but composite dish-level values are not published. 3. Potassium_mg estimated at 280mg/100g based on weighted contribution of potato (~400mg/100g cooked) and bell pepper (~210mg/100g) per USDA FoodData Central; this is an estimate and not a directly measured composite value. 4. trans_fat_g of 0.05g/100g is retained from the input record as a plausible trace value from cooking oil; confirmed as trace level consistent with USDA data for similar preparations. 5. The dish is served in Singapore as a Chinese restaurant item; country_of_origin for the cuisine is Xinjiang, China, though the specific record originates from a Singapore GrabFood listing. 6. Halal status is conditional on the restaurant using halal-certified chicken and soy sauce without alcohol-based additives; this should be verified at point of purchase. 7. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Da Pan Ji exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2026-04-16.
